title: Concrete garage floor cost in 2026: full pour pricing
excerpt: Pouring a concrete garage floor costs $5-$12 per square foot in 2026 — typically $2,500-$5,500 for a standard 2-car garage at 500-600 square feet.
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed garage floor, slab, foundation, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, pouring a concrete garage floor runs $5-$12 per square foot installed nationally, with $7-$9 per square foot the most common quote band for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh reinforcement. On a 2-car garage at 500-600 square feet, that's $2,500-$7,200, with most quotes between $3,500-$5,500. The number assumes new-construction context — pouring inside an existing garage shell, with framed walls already in place, runs 15-25 percent higher because access is constrained, equipment can't reach the floor, and finishing has to work around door openings and wall corners. Garage floor coatings (epoxy, polyaspartic, polyurea) are a separate $4-$12 per square foot install applied after the concrete cures.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to pour a 2-car garage floor in 2026?
faqs.answer: A standard 2-car garage floor (500-600 square feet) costs $2,500-$7,200 to pour in 2026, with $3,500-$5,500 the most common quote band. That's $5-$12 per square foot for a 4-inch slab with wire mesh, broom finish, and 3,000-3,500 PSI ready-mix. Replacement of an existing garage floor (demo + haul-off + pour) runs 25-40 percent higher because of the constrained access inside a finished garage shell.

faqs.question: How thick should a concrete garage floor be?
faqs.answer: 4 inches is the residential standard for a 2-car garage storing passenger vehicles. 5-6 inches is recommended for garages storing heavy trucks, RVs, or workshop equipment. Per the American Concrete Institute ACI 332 (residential code), garage floors must support the parked-vehicle load plus any equipment, with PSI typically 3,000-3,500 for standard residential and 4,000 for heavy-load garages.

faqs.question: Does a garage floor need rebar or wire mesh?
faqs.answer: Wire mesh (6x6 W2.9 welded wire fabric) is the residential standard for 2-car garage floors and is sufficient for passenger vehicle loads. Rebar is recommended for garages storing heavy trucks, RVs, or workshop equipment; for garages on expansive soils; and in freeze-thaw climates. Synthetic fiber added at the truck is a comparable alternative to mesh on standard residential floors.

faqs.question: How long does it take to pour a garage floor?
faqs.answer: Pouring a standard 2-car garage floor takes one day of on-site work: 2-3 hours for forms and base prep, 2-3 hours for the pour and finish, and 1-2 hours for control joint cuts and cleanup. The slab is walkable in 24-48 hours and vehicle-drivable at 7 days. Full design strength is reached at 28 days per the Portland Cement Association.

faqs.question: Can you pour a new concrete floor over an existing garage floor?
faqs.answer: You can with a 2-3 inch concrete overlay if the existing floor is structurally sound (no settlement, no major cracks, no contamination). Overlays run $4-$8 per square foot. The downside: an overlay raises the floor by 2-3 inches, which affects door clearances, electrical outlets, and the connection between the slab and the garage walls. Most replacement projects break out the old slab and pour fresh at the original grade.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to replace a cracked garage floor?
faqs.answer: Full garage floor replacement (demo, haul-off, base prep, new pour) runs $7-$16 per square foot in 2026 — typically $3,500-$8,500 for a 2-car garage. The replacement number is 25-40 percent higher than new construction because of constrained access inside a finished garage shell. Partial slab replacement (one section between control joints) is $700-$1,400 for a 6 x 8 ft area.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to replace a garage floor?
faqs.answer: Most jurisdictions require a permit for full garage floor replacement because the work touches the structural slab. Permits run $50-$300. New garage construction always requires a building permit that covers the floor. Slab repair (crack injection, partial section replacement, overlay) typically does not require a permit unless it crosses into structural modification.

faqs.question: What's the best finish for a garage floor?
faqs.answer: Broom finish is the residential standard for an exposed garage floor — slip-resistant, low cost, and durable. Smooth trowel finish is preferred when an epoxy or polyaspartic coating will be applied (better surface adhesion). For high-end garages or workshops, polished concrete adds $4-$8 per square foot and delivers a hard, easy-to-clean surface. Decorative finishes (stamped, stained) are uncommon on garages because they're typically covered by a vehicle or coating.

howToSteps.name: Layout and excavation
howToSteps.text: Mark the floor area inside the garage walls or pour footprint. Excavate 7-8 inches deep (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Remove topsoil and any organic material from the subgrade.

howToSteps.name: Subgrade compaction and base
howToSteps.text: Compact the exposed subgrade with a plate compactor. Place 4 inches of compacted #57 or #67 crushed stone over the prepared subgrade in 2-inch lifts. Final base is graded to slope 1-2 percent toward the door for drainage.

howToSteps.name: Install vapor barrier
howToSteps.text: Lay a 10-15 mil polyethylene vapor barrier over the compacted base. Lap seams 6 inches and seal with tape. The vapor barrier prevents moisture from migrating up through the slab — required by code in most jurisdictions for any heated or finished garage.

howToSteps.name: Set forms and place reinforcement
howToSteps.text: Set perimeter forms (2x4 or 2x6 lumber) along the slab edge. Lay wire mesh (6x6 W2.9) on chairs or dobies to land mid-slab. Vertical rebar dowels are tied at the perimeter for new-construction garages where the slab will tie into stem walls.

howToSteps.name: Pour and screed
howToSteps.text: Pour ready-mix concrete (3,000-3,500 PSI for standard 2-car, 4,000 PSI for heavy-load) to fill the forms. Screed level with a 2x4 pulled across the form tops. Bull-float to bring cream to the top and remove screed marks.

howToSteps.name: Edge, trowel, and finish
howToSteps.text: Tool the edges with a 1/4-inch radius edger. For broom finish, drag a stiff bristle broom across the surface once concrete has set to fingerprint hardness. For smooth trowel finish (preferred under coatings), float and trowel to a hard, smooth surface.

howToSteps.name: Cut control joints
howToSteps.text: Cut control joints at 8-10 foot intervals (24-30x slab thickness) within 6-18 hours of pour. Joint depth is 1/4 of slab thickness. Typical 2-car garage gets 2-4 control joints spaced for the door width and floor area.

howToSteps.name: Cure and barricade
howToSteps.text: Spray a curing compound or cover with wet-cure plastic for 7-day moisture cure. Walking permitted at 24-48 hours; light vehicle traffic at 7 days; full strength at 28 days. Wait at least 30 days before applying epoxy or polyaspartic coatings to allow moisture release.

content:
Quick Answer: A concrete garage floor costs $5-$12 per square foot to pour in 2026, with $7-$9 per square foot the most common rate. A standard 2-car garage (500-600 sq ft) runs $2,500-$7,200 total — most quotes between $3,500-$5,500. Replacement of an existing garage floor is 25-40 percent more expensive than new construction because of constrained access inside the garage shell.

Garage floors are the most-overlooked concrete pour in a residential build because they sit unseen under vehicles, equipment, and coatings — but they're also the slab that takes the hardest combination of point loads, salt exposure, and chemical contact in a typical home. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 garage floor pricing for new pour, replacement, and partial repair, plus where coatings fit in the cost stack.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed garage floor, slab, foundation, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, pouring a concrete garage floor runs $5-$12 per square foot installed nationally, with $7-$9 per square foot the most common quote band for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh reinforcement. On a 2-car garage at 500-600 square feet, that's $2,500-$7,200, with most quotes between $3,500-$5,500. The number assumes new-construction context — pouring inside an existing garage shell, with framed walls already in place, runs 15-25 percent higher because access is constrained, equipment can't reach the floor, and finishing has to work around door openings and wall corners. Garage floor coatings (epoxy, polyaspartic, polyurea) are a separate $4-$12 per square foot install applied after the concrete cures.

2026 garage floor pricing by size

National typical installed pricing for a new garage floor pour — 4-inch slab, wire mesh reinforcement, 3,000-3,500 PSI, broom finish, with vapor barrier and 4-inch compacted gravel base. Assumes new-construction access (no existing structure to work around).

Garage sizeApproximate sq ftLow ($5/sf)Typical ($8/sf)High ($12/sf)
1-car (12x20)240$1,200$1,920$2,880
1.5-car (16x22)352$1,760$2,820$4,220
2-car (20x20)400$2,000$3,200$4,800
2-car (22x24)528$2,640$4,225$6,340
2-car (24x24)576$2,880$4,610$6,910
3-car (30x24)720$3,600$5,760$8,640
3-car (32x24)768$3,840$6,150$9,220

Most homeowners with a 2-car garage budget land between $3,500 and $5,500 for the floor pour alone. That's the median 2-car garage at $7-$9 per square foot installed, before any coating, decorative finish, or heavy-duty spec upgrade.

Replacement vs new-construction pour

Pricing diverges between new-build and replacement for three reasons:

Demolition and haul-off

Existing garage floor demolition runs $1.50-$3.50 per square foot — $750-$2,000 on a typical 2-car garage. Reinforced slabs (mesh or rebar) cost 15-25 percent more to demolish than unreinforced.

Access constraints

An existing garage has walls, a door header, and often an attached structure. Concrete trucks can't drive into the garage; pumping fees ($350-$1,000) or wheelbarrow labor adds $2-$5 per square foot. Finishing equipment (power trowels) may not fit through the door, forcing hand-finish that adds finishing-labor time.

Finishing around existing elements

The new slab must tie cleanly to existing walls, door thresholds, and any utilities passing through the floor. This adds detail labor and forms — typically $1-$3 per square foot above new-build pricing.

Total garage floor replacement: $7-$16 per square foot in 2026, or roughly $3,500-$8,500 on a 2-car garage. Partial slab replacement (one section between control joints) is $700-$1,400 for a 6 x 8 ft area. For broader context on the replacement-vs-repair decision, see Cost of Concrete's concrete repair cost guide.

Spec choices that affect garage floor cost

Slab thickness

4 inches is the residential standard for 2-car garages storing passenger vehicles. Upgrade to 5 inches ($1-$1.50/sf) or 6 inches ($1.50-$2.50/sf) for garages storing heavy trucks, RVs, workshop equipment, or jacked vehicles. Per the American Concrete Institute, ACI 332 specifies minimum 4 inches for typical residential garage floors; vehicle-specific loads may push higher.

Concrete PSI

3,000 PSI is the minimum residential standard for garage floors. 3,500 PSI is typical for cold climates with road-salt exposure. 4,000 PSI is recommended for heavy-load garages, workshop floors, and any garage in salt-belt freeze-thaw regions. The per-yard cost difference is $10-$30, totaling $40-$120 on a typical 2-car garage.

Reinforcement

Wire mesh (6x6 W2.9) is the standard at no separate line item. Rebar ($0.50-$0.90/sf) is recommended for heavy-load floors and freeze-thaw climates. Synthetic fiber added at the truck ($10-$25/yd upcharge) is a comparable alternative to wire mesh on standard residential floors.

Vapor barrier

A 10-15 mil polyethylene vapor barrier under the slab is standard ($0.20-$0.40/sf). Required by most building codes for finished or heated garages. Critical if the garage will receive an epoxy or polyaspartic coating later — coatings fail rapidly over slabs without proper moisture control.

Finish

Broom finish is the residential default and is included in baseline pricing. Smooth trowel finish (preferred under coatings) is similar cost. Polished concrete is $4-$8/sf extra and delivers a hard, sealed surface ready to use without a separate coating.

Garage floor coatings: where they fit in the cost stack

Coatings are a separate trade applied after the concrete cures (minimum 30 days for full moisture release). They are the dominant garage-floor upgrade in residential and add real ongoing value: stain resistance, slip resistance, easier cleaning, and a finished aesthetic.

Coating type2026 installed costLifespanPros / cons
Acrylic sealer$1-$3 / sf3-5 yearsCheapest, easy DIY, short life
Single-coat epoxy$4-$7 / sf5-10 yearsGood chemical resistance, yellows in UV
Two-coat epoxy w/ chips$5-$9 / sf10-15 yearsDecorative flake finish, harder cure
Polyaspartic / polyurea$7-$12 / sf15-25 yearsUV-stable, fastest cure (1 day), most premium
Tile (porcelain or vinyl)$8-$16 / sf15-30 yearsAesthetic, snap-together vinyl is easiest to install

Polyaspartic and polyurea coatings (often marketed as "1-day garage floor" systems) have taken significant market share from traditional 2-coat epoxy systems over the last decade because of faster cure, UV stability, and longer service life. The per-square-foot premium is roughly 30-50 percent over epoxy with 50-100 percent longer lifespan.

How a garage floor is poured

  1. Layout and excavation. Mark the floor area inside the garage walls or pour footprint. Excavate 7-8 inches deep (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Remove topsoil and any organic material from the subgrade.
  2. Subgrade compaction and base. Compact the exposed subgrade with a plate compactor. Place 4 inches of compacted #57 or #67 crushed stone over the prepared subgrade in 2-inch lifts. Final base is graded to slope 1-2 percent toward the door for drainage.
  3. Install vapor barrier. Lay a 10-15 mil polyethylene vapor barrier over the compacted base. Lap seams 6 inches and seal with tape. The vapor barrier prevents moisture from migrating up through the slab — required by code in most jurisdictions for any heated or finished garage.
  4. Set forms and place reinforcement. Set perimeter forms (2x4 or 2x6 lumber) along the slab edge. Lay wire mesh (6x6 W2.9) on chairs or dobies to land mid-slab. Vertical rebar dowels are tied at the perimeter for new-construction garages where the slab will tie into stem walls.
  5. Pour and screed. Pour ready-mix concrete (3,000-3,500 PSI for standard 2-car, 4,000 PSI for heavy-load) to fill the forms. Screed level with a 2x4 pulled across the form tops. Bull-float to bring cream to the top and remove screed marks.
  6. Edge, trowel, and finish. Tool the edges with a 1/4-inch radius edger. For broom finish, drag a stiff bristle broom across the surface once concrete has set to fingerprint hardness. For smooth trowel finish (preferred under coatings), float and trowel to a hard, smooth surface.
  7. Cut control joints. Cut control joints at 8-10 foot intervals (24-30x slab thickness) within 6-18 hours of pour. Joint depth is 1/4 of slab thickness. Typical 2-car garage gets 2-4 control joints spaced for the door width and floor area.
  8. Cure and barricade. Spray a curing compound or cover with wet-cure plastic for 7-day moisture cure. Walking permitted at 24-48 hours; light vehicle traffic at 7 days; full strength at 28 days. Wait at least 30 days before applying epoxy or polyaspartic coatings to allow moisture release.

Common cost drivers beyond garage size

Climate

In freeze-thaw climates with road-salt exposure, garage floors should be poured at 4,000 PSI with air-entrainment (5-7 percent). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency identifies sodium chloride deicing salts as the leading cause of garage floor surface scaling in northern climates. Air-entrained mix adds $5-$15 per yard.

Floor drains

A center floor drain or trench drain adds $400-$1,200 to the pour, including the drain hardware, sloped pour around it, and any required plumbing connections.

Slope to door

Standard garage floor slope is 1-2 percent (1/8 to 1/4 inch per foot) toward the door for drainage. Garages on sloped lots or where the door isn't on the low side may need a custom slope plan, adding $1-$3 per square foot.

Hydronic radiant heat

Embedding hydronic radiant heat tubing in the slab adds $4-$8 per square foot to the pour, plus boiler/manifold costs. Common in detached workshop garages in cold climates.

Regional pricing variation

Garage floor pour pricing varies 25-40 percent between U.S. metros. Cheapest: South-Central U.S. at $5-$8 per square foot. Most expensive: San Francisco Bay, Seattle, Honolulu, Boston at $9-$15 per square foot. For full residential concrete cost context, see Cost of Concrete's concrete per cubic yard guide.

Frequently asked questions

How much does it cost to pour a 2-car garage floor in 2026?

A standard 2-car garage floor (500-600 square feet) costs $2,500-$7,200 to pour in 2026, with $3,500-$5,500 the most common quote band. That's $5-$12 per square foot for a 4-inch slab with wire mesh, broom finish, and 3,000-3,500 PSI ready-mix. Replacement of an existing garage floor (demo + haul-off + pour) runs 25-40 percent higher because of the constrained access inside a finished garage shell.

How thick should a concrete garage floor be?

4 inches is the residential standard for a 2-car garage storing passenger vehicles. 5-6 inches is recommended for garages storing heavy trucks, RVs, or workshop equipment. Per the American Concrete Institute ACI 332 (residential code), garage floors must support the parked-vehicle load plus any equipment, with PSI typically 3,000-3,500 for standard residential and 4,000 for heavy-load garages.

Wire mesh (6x6 W2.9 welded wire fabric) is the residential standard for 2-car garage floors and is sufficient for passenger vehicle loads. Rebar is recommended for garages storing heavy trucks, RVs, or workshop equipment; for garages on expansive soils; and in freeze-thaw climates. Synthetic fiber added at the truck is a comparable alternative to mesh on standard residential floors.

Pouring a standard 2-car garage floor takes one day of on-site work: 2-3 hours for forms and base prep, 2-3 hours for the pour and finish, and 1-2 hours for control joint cuts and cleanup. The slab is walkable in 24-48 hours and vehicle-drivable at 7 days. Full design strength is reached at 28 days per the Portland Cement Association.

You can with a 2-3 inch concrete overlay if the existing floor is structurally sound (no settlement, no major cracks, no contamination). Overlays run $4-$8 per square foot. The downside: an overlay raises the floor by 2-3 inches, which affects door clearances, electrical outlets, and the connection between the slab and the garage walls. Most replacement projects break out the old slab and pour fresh at the original grade.

Full garage floor replacement (demo, haul-off, base prep, new pour) runs $7-$16 per square foot in 2026 — typically $3,500-$8,500 for a 2-car garage. The replacement number is 25-40 percent higher than new construction because of constrained access inside a finished garage shell. Partial slab replacement (one section between control joints) is $700-$1,400 for a 6 x 8 ft area.

New garage construction always requires a building permit that covers the floor. Slab repair (crack injection, partial section replacement, overlay) typically does not require a permit unless it crosses into structural modification.

What's the best finish for a garage floor?

Broom finish is the residential standard for an exposed garage floor — slip-resistant, low cost, and durable. Smooth trowel finish is preferred when an epoxy or polyaspartic coating will be applied (better surface adhesion). For high-end garages or workshops, polished concrete adds $4-$8 per square foot and delivers a hard, easy-to-clean surface. Decorative finishes (stamped, stained) are uncommon on garages because they're typically covered by a vehicle or coating.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 national pricing: $5-$12 per square foot to pour, $7-$9 most common. Typical 2-car garage floor: $3,500-$5,500.
  • Replacement is 25-40 percent more expensive than new construction because of constrained access inside a finished garage shell.
  • Standard residential spec: 4-inch slab, 4-inch compacted gravel base, vapor barrier, wire mesh, 3,000-3,500 PSI, broom finish.
  • Upgrade to 5-6 inch slab and 4,000 PSI for heavy-load garages (trucks, RVs, workshop equipment).
  • Garage floor coatings are a separate trade — polyaspartic/polyurea ($7-$12/sf, 15-25 year life) is displacing traditional epoxy in premium residential.
  • Wait 30+ days after the pour before applying any coating to allow proper moisture release.
